macro_events.event_summary: India is ramping up coal-fired power generation as a heatwave drives record electricity demand and the Iran war tightens global LNG supply, pushing gas prices higher and making coal the marginal fuel.
primary_drivers: LNG supply disruption from Iran/Middle East conflict raising global gas prices
Coal substitution for gas in power generation when gas becomes uneconomic
Tight seaborne thermal coal market as India increases imports

beneficiaries.rationale: Peabody Energy is the largest US thermal coal producer with seaborne export exposure; tighter global thermal coal market from Indian import demand directly lifts Newcastle/API benchmark prices and BTU's export realizations.
Alpha Metallurgical primarily exports met coal but global coal price complex moves together; spillover from thermal tightness lifts overall coal equity sentiment and pricing.
Transocean and offshore drillers — wait, replacing — Teekay Tankers benefit from longer-haul LNG and coal shipping routes; rerouted energy flows lift tanker tonne-miles.
Cheniere Energy is the largest US LNG exporter; Iran-driven supply loss and elevated JKM/TTF spot prices increase the value of its uncontracted volumes and marketing margins.
Largest US natgas producer with leverage to Henry Hub strength as US LNG export demand intensifies on global gas tightness from the Iran war.
Hallador Energy is a small-cap Illinois Basin thermal coal producer — high-beta play on US thermal coal price increases as global tightness backs up into domestic markets.

losers.rationale: First Solar — actually a stretch here; Indian utilities pivoting to coal for reliability under heat stress is a marginal negative for solar-as-baseload narrative, but FSLR has limited India revenue. Low conviction loser.
ICICI Bank — Indian banks face indirect headwind if power-sector NPLs rise on coal cost inflation and if rupee weakens forcing RBI to defend currency rather than cut rates.

headlines: India is burning more coal as extreme heat and the Iran war squeeze energy supplies

macro_events.event_summary: Trump threatens new auto tariffs, pressuring European markets and foreign automakers ahead of the open.
primary_drivers: Tariff cost passthrough on imported vehicles raising effective prices for foreign OEMs
Relative competitive advantage shift toward US-domiciled auto manufacturers
Supply chain disruption risk for cross-border auto parts flows

beneficiaries.rationale: Ford has the highest share of US-built vehicles among Detroit Three; tariffs on European/imported autos make domestically produced F-150, Explorer relatively cheaper vs European luxury/mass imports.
Predominantly North American production footprint; tariffs raise sticker price of European import competitors (BMW, Mercedes, VW Audi), boosting GM's relative pricing power in US market.
100% US-manufactured for US market; direct beneficiary as European EV imports (Porsche Taycan, Audi e-tron, Mercedes EQ line) become more expensive, removing premium EV competition.
US-only manufacturing in Normal, IL with no European import exposure; benefits from any pricing umbrella raised by tariffed European premium SUVs/trucks.

losers.rationale: Stellantis has heavy European manufacturing exposure (Fiat, Peugeot, Alfa Romeo, Maserati imports to US); tariffs directly hit cost structure on EU-built models shipped stateside.

Although Japanese, Toyota imports a meaningful share of vehicles (Land Cruiser, some Lexus) and faces risk that 'auto tariffs' broaden beyond Europe to all imports as Trump tariff rhetoric historically expands.

Aptiv has substantial European manufacturing footprint for wiring/electrical systems shipped into global auto supply chains; tariffs disrupt cross-border parts flows and pressure OEM customer volumes.
Magna International is a major auto parts supplier with European plants serving European OEMs that ship to US; volume declines at BMW/Mercedes/VW US sales flow back through Magna's order book.

headlines: European markets set to open lower as Trump threatens new auto tariffs
